note: A wider set of public-finance and economic indicators. Status (rising/falling/stable) describes the recent direction of the figure itself, not whether that direction is good or bad — context matters and is left to the reader. Headline values were verified against ONS and OBR releases in June 2026; revenue and spending totals are estimates.

indicators.name: Debt as a share of GDP
indicators.explanation: Net debt measured against the size of the economy.

indicators.name: Borrowing as a share of GDP
indicators.explanation: Annual public sector net borrowing relative to the economy.

indicators.name: Debt interest
indicators.explanation: The cost of servicing debt, relative to the economy. About £111 billion a year.

indicators.name: Inflation (CPI)
indicators.explanation: The rate at which consumer prices are rising over the year. Eased in April as household energy bills fell.
indicators.source_name: ONS, Consumer price inflation, UK: April 2026

indicators.name: Unemployment rate
indicators.explanation: The share of people who want to work and are looking for a job but cannot find one. Up from 4.5% a year earlier.

indicators.name: GDP growth
indicators.explanation: How fast the economy is growing, after adjusting for inflation. The 2026 forecast, down from 1.4% in 2025.

indicators.name: Productivity (output per hour)
indicators.explanation: How much is produced per hour worked, versus a year earlier. Weak productivity growth has been a long-running UK challenge.

indicators.name: Tax burden
indicators.explanation: Total taxes as a share of the economy. Forecast to keep rising to the highest level since records began in 1948.

indicators.name: Government revenue
indicators.explanation: Total money the government receives, mostly from taxes.

indicators.name: Government spending
indicators.explanation: Total money the government spends on services, benefits, investment, and debt interest.
